name: sql
context: SQL language standard — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, JOINs, aggregations, CTEs, and window functions
info: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for interacting with relational databases. This node covers the full breadth of SQL used in application development: CRUD operations (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), JOIN types for combining tables, aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) with GROUP BY, Common Table Expressions (CTEs) for readable complex queries, window functions for ranking and running totals, and transaction control for data integrity. Examples target PostgreSQL syntax but note dialect differences for MySQL and SQLite where relevant.
body:
# SQL Language

SQL is the standard language for querying and manipulating relational databases. Every [[database]] engine supports SQL with minor dialect variations. This reference covers the operations agents use most: CRUD, JOINs, aggregations, CTEs, window functions, and transactions.

## CRUD Operations

### SELECT

```sql
-- Basic select with filtering and ordering
SELECT id, name, email, created_at
FROM users
WHERE status = 'active'
  AND created_at > '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 0;

-- Select with alias and expression
SELECT
  u.name,
  COUNT(o.id) AS order_count,
  SUM(o.total) AS lifetime_value
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.name
HAVING SUM(o.total) > 1000;
```

### INSERT

```sql
-- Single row
INSERT INTO users (name, email, status)
VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com', 'active')
RETURNING id, created_at;

-- Multiple rows
INSERT INTO tags (name, slug)
VALUES
  ('JavaScript', 'javascript'),
  ('TypeScript', 'typescript'),
  ('Python', 'python');

-- Insert from select
INSERT INTO user_archive (id, name, email)
SELECT id, name, email FROM users WHERE status = 'deleted';
```

### UPDATE

```sql
-- Update with condition
UPDATE users
SET status = 'inactive', updated_at = NOW()
WHERE last_login < NOW() - INTERVAL '1 year'
RETURNING id, name;

-- Update with join (PostgreSQL)
UPDATE orders o
SET discount = 0.10
FROM users u
WHERE o.user_id = u.id AND u.tier = 'premium';
```

### DELETE

```sql
-- Delete with condition
DELETE FROM sessions
WHERE expires_at < NOW();

-- Delete with subquery
DELETE FROM orders
WHERE user_id IN (
  SELECT id FROM users WHERE status = 'banned'
);
```

## JOINs

```sql
-- INNER JOIN — only matching rows
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- LEFT JOIN — all left rows, matching right rows or NULL
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- RIGHT JOIN — all right rows, matching left rows or NULL
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
RIGHT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN — all rows from both sides
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;

-- CROSS JOIN — cartesian product
SELECT colors.name, sizes.name
FROM colors CROSS JOIN sizes;

-- Self JOIN
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
```

## Aggregations

```sql
-- Group by with aggregate functions
SELECT
  category,
  COUNT(*) AS product_count,
  AVG(price) AS avg_price,
  MIN(price) AS min_price,
  MAX(price) AS max_price,
  SUM(quantity) AS total_stock
FROM products
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;

-- Group by with ROLLUP (subtotals)
SELECT
  COALESCE(region, 'ALL REGIONS') AS region,
  COALESCE(category, 'ALL CATEGORIES') AS category,
  SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
GROUP BY ROLLUP(region, category);
```

## Common Table Expressions (CTEs)

```sql
-- Basic CTE for readability
WITH active_users AS (
  SELECT id, name, email
  FROM users
  WHERE status = 'active'
),
recent_orders AS (
  SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(total) AS total_spent
  FROM orders
  WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  au.name,
  au.email,
  ro.order_count,
  ro.total_spent
FROM active_users au
JOIN recent_orders ro ON ro.user_id = au.id
ORDER BY ro.total_spent DESC;

-- Recursive CTE — tree traversal
WITH RECURSIVE category_tree AS (
  SELECT id, name, parent_id, 0 AS depth
  FROM categories
  WHERE parent_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT c.id, c.name, c.parent_id, ct.depth + 1
  FROM categories c
  JOIN category_tree ct ON c.parent_id = ct.id
)
SELECT * FROM category_tree ORDER BY depth, name;
```

## Window Functions

```sql
-- ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK
SELECT
  name,
  department,
  salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS row_num,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank
FROM employees;

-- Running total and moving average
SELECT
  date,
  revenue,
  SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_total,
  AVG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_7d
FROM daily_sales;

-- LAG and LEAD
SELECT
  date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day,
  revenue - LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS day_over_day
FROM daily_sales;
```

## Transactions

```sql
-- Basic transaction with ACID guarantees
BEGIN;

UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;

-- Verify both updates succeeded
SELECT id, balance FROM accounts WHERE id IN (1, 2);

COMMIT;

-- Rollback on error
BEGIN;
UPDATE inventory SET quantity = quantity - 1 WHERE product_id = 42;
-- If something goes wrong:
ROLLBACK;
```

See [[database#acid-properties]] for details on transaction isolation levels. For indexing queries to run faster, see [[database/indexing]]. For managing schema changes over time, see [[database/migrations]].

sections.title: CRUD operations
sections.body: SELECT retrieves data with optional WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT, and OFFSET. INSERT adds rows individually or in bulk, with optional RETURNING (PostgreSQL). UPDATE modifies existing rows, supports joins for multi-table updates. DELETE removes rows matching a condition. Always use WHERE with UPDATE and DELETE to avoid full-table modifications. Use RETURNING to get affected row data without a separate SELECT.

sections.title: JOIN types
sections.body: INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all left rows with NULLs for non-matching right rows. RIGHT JOIN is the inverse. FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both sides. CROSS JOIN produces a cartesian product. Self JOINs connect a table to itself (e.g., employees to managers). Choose the join type based on whether you need unmatched rows. See [[database/indexing]] for ensuring joins use indexes efficiently.

sections.title: Aggregate functions
sections.body: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX operate on groups defined by GROUP BY. HAVING filters groups after aggregation (WHERE filters rows before). Use ROLLUP for subtotals and CUBE for cross-tabulation. DISTINCT inside aggregates counts unique values: `COUNT(DISTINCT category)`. FILTER clause (PostgreSQL) applies conditions per-aggregate: `COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE status = 'active')`.

sections.title: Common Table Expressions
sections.body: CTEs (WITH clauses) name subqueries for readability and reuse within a statement. Non-recursive CTEs simplify complex joins and multi-step transformations. Recursive CTEs traverse hierarchical data (trees, graphs) using UNION ALL with a base case and recursive step. CTEs can be chained: `WITH a AS (...), b AS (SELECT ... FROM a) SELECT ... FROM b`. In PostgreSQL, CTEs are optimization fences by default — use `WITH ... AS MATERIALIZED` or `NOT MATERIALIZED` to control.

sections.title: Window functions
sections.body: Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them. ROW_NUMBER assigns sequential numbers. RANK and DENSE_RANK handle ties differently. SUM/AVG/COUNT as window functions produce running totals and moving averages. LAG and LEAD access previous and next rows. FIRST_VALUE and LAST_VALUE access boundary rows. Frame clauses (ROWS BETWEEN, RANGE BETWEEN) control the window size. PARTITION BY groups the window; ORDER BY defines ordering within each partition.

sections.title: Transactions and isolation
sections.body: Transactions group operations into atomic units: BEGIN starts, COMMIT persists, ROLLBACK aborts. Isolation levels control visibility of concurrent changes: READ UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads), READ COMMITTED (PostgreSQL default), REPEATABLE READ (MySQL InnoDB default), SERIALIZABLE (strictest). Use `SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL` to change. Savepoints (`SAVEPOINT sp1; ROLLBACK TO sp1`) enable partial rollbacks within a transaction. See [[database#acid-properties]] for theoretical background.

links.context: Database ecosystem overview and engine comparison
Manage schema changes that affect query structure
ORMs generate SQL — understanding raw SQL helps debug ORM output
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to understand query execution plans

related.note: Indexes determine whether queries scan full tables or use efficient lookups
ORMs abstract SQL but understanding raw SQL is essential for debugging
Schema changes via migrations alter the tables SQL queries target

author: dojo-community
